_meta.description: Data Pilots Trust Check — canonical registry of all checks. Every check maps to both a Framework Area (expert/compliance view) and a Trust Pillar (buyer-facing product view).

journey.aware: Free tier — surface checks on the public website. What a buyer or auditor can see without asking.
journey.prepared: Paid tier — depth within each framework area. Requires account. Results saved and trackable.
journey.prepared_plus: Paid Plus — operational checks, human review, portal/platform scan, remediation support.

framework_areas.transparency: Do you clearly tell people what you do with their data — before you do it?
framework_areas.storing_securing: Is the data you hold protected — technically and procedurally?
framework_areas.sharing_data: Do you know what data leaves your organisation, where it goes, and on what terms?
framework_areas.responsible_use: Are you using data (including AI) only in ways people would reasonably expect and accept?
framework_areas.individual_rights: Can people actually exercise their rights — access, correction, deletion, opt-out?

trust_pillars.transparency: Do you clearly tell buyers and users what you do with their data?
trust_pillars.safety: Is your site technically secure and protected against known vulnerabilities?
trust_pillars.ux: Can buyers find what they need to feel confident before saying yes?
trust_pillars.ai_trust: Are you transparent about how you use AI — for buyers and for learners?

checks.name: HTTPS / Secure Connection
checks.what_we_check: Whether the site loads over HTTPS
checks.why_it_matters_business: Browsers mark HTTP sites as 'Not Secure'. Any buyer who sees that label has immediate doubt before the conversation even starts.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Encryption in transit is required under GDPR Art. 32 (appropriate technical measures) and is a baseline for ISO 27001 and Cyber Essentials.
checks.fix_guide: Contact your hosting provider and enable an SSL certificate. Most modern hosts (Squarespace, Wix, WordPress) do this for free in one click.

checks.name: HSTS (HTTP Strict Transport Security)
checks.what_we_check: Whether the Strict-Transport-Security header is present
checks.why_it_matters_business: HSTS tells browsers to always use HTTPS — even on the first connection. Without it, a sophisticated attacker can intercept that first load.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Aligns with GDPR Art. 32, Cyber Essentials, and NIST PR.DS (protect data in transit).
checks.fix_guide: Add `Strict-Transport-Security: max-age=31536000; includeSubDomains` to your server or CDN headers. Many hosting dashboards have a one-click option.

checks.name: Content Security Policy (CSP)
checks.what_we_check: Whether a Content-Security-Policy header is set
checks.why_it_matters_business: CSP prevents malicious scripts being injected into your site. Procurement security questionnaires explicitly ask about this.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Addresses GDPR Art. 32 technical safeguards, ISO 27001 A.8 (application security), SOC 2 CC6.
checks.fix_guide: Start with a basic policy via your CDN (Cloudflare, Netlify) or hosting headers. A developer can set a starter policy in under an hour.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you have a developer who manages your hosting and server config?
Are you on a CDN like Cloudflare that allows header rules without code changes?

checks.name: Clickjacking Protection
checks.what_we_check: X-Frame-Options or CSP frame-ancestors directive
checks.why_it_matters_business: Prevents your site being embedded invisibly inside a malicious page to trick users.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: ISO 27001 A.8 (application security), Cyber Essentials.
checks.fix_guide: Add `X-Frame-Options: SAMEORIGIN` header, or use `frame-ancestors 'self'` in your CSP.

checks.name: Privacy Policy Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a privacy policy page exists at a standard path
checks.why_it_matters_business: 73% of L&D buyers check for a privacy policy before signing. Missing = immediate red flag in procurement.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Required under GDPR Art. 13/14 (information to be provided), UK DPA 2018, and CCPA §1798.100.
checks.fix_guide: Create a /privacy-policy page. If you're not sure what to include, use a GDPR-compliant generator (e.g. iubenda, Termly) as a starting point — then customise to reflect how you actually use data.
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you had your privacy policy reviewed by a solicitor or data protection advisor?
Is your privacy policy the same for website visitors and for your learners/clients?
When was your privacy policy last reviewed or updated?

checks.name: GDPR Language in Privacy Policy
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy includes GDPR-specific concepts: lawful basis, data subject rights, data controller, supervisory authority
checks.why_it_matters_business: UK and EU enterprise procurement teams will reject suppliers whose privacy policy doesn't reference GDPR. It signals data protection hasn't been taken seriously.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 13/14 requires specific disclosures including lawful basis, data subject rights, and supervisory authority details.
checks.fix_guide: Update your privacy policy to explicitly name: your lawful basis for processing, data subject rights (access, erasure, portability, objection), and the ICO as supervisory authority (UK).
follow_up_questions_paid: Are all your clients UK or EU-based, or do you have US/global clients too?
Have you registered with the ICO (UK) as a data controller?
Do you have a named Data Protection Officer or responsible person?

checks.name: Privacy Policy — Required Topics
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy covers: what is collected, why, third-party sharing, visitor rights, and contact details
checks.why_it_matters_business: A policy that skips any of these isn't just incomplete — it's unlikely to satisfy enterprise procurement requirements.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 13 and 14 require all five topics to be addressed for lawful processing.
checks.fix_guide: Review your policy against each topic and add a section for any that are missing. Plain language works better than legal boilerplate for both buyers and regulators.

checks.name: Privacy Policy — Last Updated Date
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy shows a visible 'last updated' or 'effective date'
checks.why_it_matters_business: Policies without a date look stale. Buyers notice — it signals your data governance hasn't been reviewed recently.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Good practice under GDPR accountability principle (Art. 5(2)); expected by ISO 27001 document control.
checks.fix_guide: Add 'Last updated: [date]' to the top of your privacy policy. Review and re-date it at least annually.

checks.name: CCPA / US Privacy Language
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy addresses CCPA rights: do not sell, right to know, right to delete
checks.why_it_matters_business: If you have US-based clients (especially California), CCPA compliance is legally required and increasingly expected in enterprise procurement.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: California Consumer Privacy Act §1798.100 et seq. Applies to businesses processing California residents' data above certain thresholds.
checks.fix_guide: Add a CCPA section to your privacy policy covering the right to know, right to delete, and opt-out of sale. Link it from your footer.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you have clients or learners based in California or other US states?
Do you sell or share personal data with third parties for advertising purposes?

checks.name: Data Processing Agreement Language
checks.what_we_check: Whether the privacy policy or site references Data Processing Agreements, sub-processors, or standard contractual clauses
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise and public sector clients almost always require a signed DPA before a contract is issued. If it's not on your radar, deals get stuck at legal sign-off.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28 requires a written contract (DPA) between controller and processor. Standard Contractual Clauses required for international transfers.
checks.fix_guide: Draft a standard DPA template you can offer to enterprise clients. List your sub-processors. A data consultant or solicitor can help draft this in a day.
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you ever been asked to sign a Data Processing Agreement by a client?
Do you have a standard DPA template you can share with clients?
What tools do you use that process learner or client personal data (LMS, video platform, email tool)?
Are any of your tools US-based? (relevant for international data transfer rules)

checks.name: Terms of Service / Use Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a terms of service or terms and conditions page exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise procurement requires clear terms before signing. A missing terms page can stall a deal at the contract stage.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: Required for clarity on data responsibilities and liability. Referenced in GDPR Art. 28 controller-processor relationships.
checks.fix_guide: Create a /terms or /terms-of-service page covering: what the service includes, payment terms, IP ownership, liability limits, and cancellation policy.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you have standard terms you send clients, or do you negotiate bespoke contracts?
Are your terms different for individual buyers vs enterprise/public sector?
Do your terms reference GDPR obligations or data handling?

checks.name: Dedicated Cookie Policy / Disclosure Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a dedicated cookie policy or cookie disclosure page exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: Many privacy-aware buyers check for this specifically. A missing cookie disclosure is one of the first things flagged in a privacy audit.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR and the UK PECR require clear disclosure of cookies used, their purpose, duration, and how to opt out.
checks.fix_guide: Create a /cookie-policy page listing each cookie category, the specific cookies used, their purpose and duration, and how to opt out.

checks.name: Policy Links in Footer
checks.what_we_check: Whether Privacy Policy and Terms links are visible in the site footer
checks.why_it_matters_business: Buyers look in the footer first. Not finding policy links there creates doubt before they've read anything.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR requires privacy information to be 'easily accessible'. Footer placement is the recognised standard.
checks.fix_guide: Add Privacy Policy and Terms of Service links to your footer navigation. They should be visible on every page.

checks.name: Cookie Policy Link in Footer
checks.what_we_check: Whether a cookie policy link is visible in the footer
checks.why_it_matters_business: Privacy-aware buyers and regulators expect cookie disclosures to be easy to find, not buried.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: UK PECR and GDPR require cookie information to be accessible. Footer is the standard location.
checks.fix_guide: Add a 'Cookie Policy' link to your footer alongside your Privacy Policy.

checks.name: Contact Page
checks.what_we_check: Whether a contact or about page exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: If buyers can't find how to contact you, they lose confidence. Visible contact details are a basic trust signal.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR requires a way for data subjects to reach the data controller to exercise their rights (Art. 12). Contact details are mandatory in the privacy policy.
checks.fix_guide: Make sure you have a /contact or /about page with a working email, phone number, or contact form. A physical or registered address adds credibility for enterprise buyers.
follow_up_questions_paid: Do you include your registered business address on your site?
Do you have a named individual (not just info@) as a primary point of contact for data queries?

checks.name: Cookie Consent Language (HTML scan)
checks.what_we_check: Whether cookie, GDPR, consent, or data protection language appears in the page HTML
checks.why_it_matters_business: No consent language is often a sign there's no consent mechanism at all — one of the most common trust failures we find.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR and UK PECR require freely given, specific, informed, and unambiguous consent before non-essential cookies fire.
checks.fix_guide: Implement a consent management platform (CMP) such as Cookiebot, CookieYes, or OneTrust. Configure it to block non-essential cookies until the visitor explicitly accepts.

checks.name: Consent Banner — Live Browser Check
checks.what_we_check: Whether a real browser agent visiting your site sees a consent banner with Accept, Decline, and Settings options
checks.why_it_matters_business: An HTML keyword scan can miss a banner loaded via JavaScript. Papaya's agent sees what a real buyer sees — including whether the choice is genuinely there.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR and UK PECR require a clear, accessible mechanism for consent and withdrawal. The ICO has fined organisations for non-functional consent banners.
checks.fix_guide: Implement a CMP. Ensure the banner appears on first load, before any non-essential cookies fire.

checks.name: Reject-All Effectiveness — Live Browser Check
checks.what_we_check: Whether clicking 'Decline All' actually stops tracking — comparing tracker counts before and after
checks.why_it_matters_business: Many sites have a Decline All button that doesn't work. This is one of the most common GDPR enforcement findings — and a serious liability in an enterprise procurement review.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR requires withdrawal of consent to be as easy as giving it (Art. 7(3)). A non-functional reject button is a direct violation. The ICO and EU DPAs have issued significant fines for this.
checks.fix_guide: Work with your CMP provider to ensure Decline All actually blocks cookie firing — not just hides the banner. Test with a browser network inspector.

checks.name: Pre-Consent Tracking Volume — Live Browser Check
checks.what_we_check: Number of trackers firing on page load before any consent is given
checks.why_it_matters_business: High pre-consent tracker counts are a direct compliance risk and the kind of finding that surfaces immediately in a procurement privacy audit.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR requires consent before non-essential processing begins (Art. 6). Pre-consent tracking is prima facie evidence of unlawful processing.
checks.fix_guide: Configure your CMP to block all non-essential scripts from loading until consent is given. Move script tags to the CMP's 'on-accept' category.

checks.name: Consent Management Platform Identified
checks.what_we_check: Whether Papaya's report identifies a recognised CMP (OneTrust, Cookiebot, etc.)
checks.why_it_matters_business: Using a recognised CMP signals mature consent governance. Enterprise procurement teams often verify this.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: ICO guidance recommends using a recognised CMP. Required for IAB TCF compliance if working with advertising networks.
checks.fix_guide: Consider Cookiebot, CookieYes, or OneTrust depending on your budget and scale.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which consent management platform are you currently using?
Does your CMP log consent records so you can evidence compliance if audited?

checks.name: Consent Category Granularity
checks.what_we_check: Whether the consent banner offers granular categories (analytics, marketing, functional) vs a single accept/reject
checks.why_it_matters_business: A blanket accept/reject without categories may not be legally sufficient — and signals a less mature consent setup to procurement teams.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR requires specific consent per purpose (Art. 6). A blanket consent does not meet the 'specific' requirement. ICO enforcement has focused on this.
checks.fix_guide: Configure your CMP to offer separate consent toggles for at least: Strictly Necessary, Analytics/Performance, and Marketing/Advertising.

checks.name: AI Tool Disclosure (Public Site)
checks.what_we_check: Whether the site mentions AI tools, partners, or AI-powered features
checks.why_it_matters_business: Over half of enterprise L&D buyers now ask about AI use before signing. A proactive disclosure prevents the question becoming a procurement blocker.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 22 requires disclosure of automated decision-making. EU AI Act (2026+) will require transparency for high-risk AI uses in education/employment contexts.
checks.fix_guide: Add a short, plain-language note on your website describing how and where you use AI — and importantly, what data is involved.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which AI tools do you use in your L&D delivery or content creation?
Does any AI tool you use process learner data (names, scores, chat history)?
Do your client contracts or terms mention AI use?
Have any clients asked you to complete an AI risk assessment or questionnaire?

checks.name: Sitemap
checks.what_we_check: Whether a sitemap.xml exists
checks.why_it_matters_business: A sitemap is a minor signal of a well-maintained, professionally run site.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: No direct compliance requirement, but supports accessibility and discoverability.
checks.fix_guide: Most website builders generate sitemaps automatically. Check your platform settings.

checks.name: Learner Data Notice in LMS / Portal
checks.what_we_check: Whether your learning portal or platform shows learners a data notice at point of collection
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise HR and legal teams will ask: 'What are your learners told about how their data is used?' You need a clear answer — and a working notice.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 13 requires information to be provided 'at the time personal data are obtained'. Learner completion records, quiz scores, and activity logs are personal data.
checks.fix_guide: Add a learner-facing data notice to your LMS registration or onboarding flow. Explain what's collected, why, how long it's kept, and how learners can request access or deletion.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which LMS or learning platform do you use?
What learner data do you collect? (completion, quiz scores, time-on-task, etc.)
Who can access individual learner data — your team, the learner, or the client employer?
How long do you retain learner completion and activity records?

checks.name: Learner Data Retention Policy
checks.what_we_check: Whether you have a documented retention period for learner data
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients will ask 'how long do you keep our employees' data?' — you need a clear, documented answer.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR storage limitation principle (Art. 5(1)(e)) requires data to be kept no longer than necessary. Must be documented under accountability principle.
checks.fix_guide: Define and document your retention periods (e.g. completion records kept 3 years, then deleted or anonymised). Include in your privacy policy and DPA.
follow_up_questions_paid: How long do you currently keep learner completion and activity data?
Is there a process to delete or anonymise data on request?
Do you have a data deletion schedule, or is it ad hoc?

checks.name: Third-Party Tools in Learning Delivery
checks.what_we_check: What third-party tools process learner data (video platforms, LMS, email tools, analytics)
checks.why_it_matters_business: Every third-party tool that touches learner data is a sub-processor. Enterprise clients will ask for this list during procurement.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28 requires sub-processors to be disclosed and contracted. Clients have the right to object to sub-processor changes.
checks.fix_guide: Make a list of every tool that processes learner or client personal data. Ensure each has a DPA with you. List them as sub-processors in your privacy policy.
follow_up_questions_paid: Which video conferencing tool do you use for live learning? (Zoom, Teams, etc.)
Which LMS or platform hosts your content?
Do you use any AI-powered tools that learners interact with directly?
Do you use email marketing tools to communicate with learners or clients?

checks.name: AI Disclosure to Learners
checks.what_we_check: Whether learners are told when AI is involved in their learning experience (AI tutor, AI-generated content, AI-based scoring)
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients are increasingly asking about this — and regulation is catching up fast, especially in education and employment contexts.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 22 (automated decision-making), EU AI Act provisions for high-risk AI in education/employment, emerging ICO guidance on AI transparency.
checks.fix_guide: Add an AI disclosure to your learner onboarding. Be specific about what AI does in their experience and whether it affects assessment or personalisation.
follow_up_questions_paid: Does your platform use AI for personalisation, recommendation, or automated feedback?
Are learners ever assessed by an AI system (not a human)?
Is AI-generated content used in any of your courses? Is this disclosed?

checks.name: Internal AI Governance Policy
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business has a documented policy for responsible AI use
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients in regulated sectors are now asking suppliers for evidence of AI governance. A one-page AI policy is often enough to pass initial procurement screening.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: EU AI Act requires governance for high-risk AI. ICO AI guidance recommends documented accountability. ISO 42001 (AI Management System) emerging standard.
checks.fix_guide: Draft a short AI use policy covering: which tools you use, what data they access, how outputs are quality-checked, and who is responsible for AI decisions.
follow_up_questions_paid: Does your team have formal guidance on which AI tools are approved for use?
Have any enterprise clients asked you to complete an AI risk assessment?

checks.name: Standard DPA Available for Clients
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business has a signed or signable DPA template ready for enterprise clients
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise and public sector clients almost universally require a signed DPA before awarding a contract. Not having one ready delays or kills deals.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28 — mandatory written agreement between controller and processor. Absence = non-compliant processing.
checks.fix_guide: Work with a data protection solicitor to create a standard DPA naming you as data processor, listing sub-processors, defining retention periods, and referencing your security measures.
follow_up_questions_paid: Have you signed a DPA with any of your current clients?
Do you have a solicitor or data protection advisor you work with?
Are any of your clients public sector or regulated industry (NHS, financial services, education)?

checks.name: Sub-Processor List Published
checks.what_we_check: Whether the business publishes or can provide a list of sub-processors
checks.why_it_matters_business: Enterprise clients will ask for this list during procurement. Not having it ready creates delays and signals immature data governance.
checks.why_it_matters_compliance: GDPR Art. 28(3)(d) — processor must provide controller with information about sub-processors. Controllers have right to object to new sub-processors.
checks.fix_guide: Create a sub-processor list with: tool name, purpose, data location, link to their privacy/security page. Keep it updated when you add new tools.
follow_up_questions_paid: Can you list the key tools your business uses that handle client or learner data?
Are any of these tools based outside the UK/EU? (affects international transfer rules)
